title: Add Elapsed Minutes to a Start Time
description: Add one or more minute intervals to a given start time to find an end time.
condition: Given a start time and one or more durations in minutes to be added.
response: Add the minutes to the start time, updating the hour as needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes regrouping when total minutes exceed 60. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Add Multiple Time Durations
description: Add several time intervals (including play time and break time) to determine a final end time.
condition: Given a start time and multiple durations in minutes that occur sequentially.
response: Add all durations to the start time, regrouping minutes into hours as needed, to find the final time.
label: Principle / Model
example: Start at 5:25 p.m.; durations: $17 + 12 + 13 = 42$ minutes. \ $5{:}25 + 42\text{ minutes} = 6{:}07$ p.m.
notes: Requires regrouping when total minutes exceed 60.

title: Add Two Categories from Graph
description: Add the values of two categories read from a graph to find a combined total.
condition: Two category values are identified from a bar graph and the total of both is requested.
response: Compute the sum of the two category values.
label: Principle
example: If tacos = 160 and salad = 80, then total = $160 + 80 = 240$.

title: Classify Quadrilateral by Properties
description: Determine whether a quadrilateral meets or does not meet given property constraints.
condition: Given a quadrilateral and conditions such as 'not a rectangle' and 'at least one right angle.'
response: Produce or identify a quadrilateral that satisfies all stated properties.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Draw a quadrilateral with one $90^\circ$ angle but with sides not forming two pairs of parallel sides (so it is not a rectangle).
notes: Construction may vary; multiple correct answers.

title: Compute Elapsed Time Across an Hour
description: Find the number of minutes between two times when the interval crosses from one hour to the next.
condition: Given a start time and end time where the end minutes are less than the start minutes (crossing an hour).
response: Count up to the next hour, then add remaining minutes to the end time.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $11{:}55$ a.m. to $12{:}35$ p.m.: $5$ minutes to $12{:}00$ plus $35$ minutes = $40$ minutes.

title: Compute Perimeter by Summing All Side Lengths
description: To find the perimeter of any polygon, add the lengths of all its exterior sides.
condition: Given a polygon with all side lengths labeled.
response: Add all side lengths to obtain the total perimeter.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a polygon has sides 6 cm, 4 cm, 8 cm, 6 cm, 14 cm, and 10 cm, then its perimeter is $6 + 4 + 8 + 6 + 14 + 10 = 48$ cm.
notes: Applies to irregular polygons as well as regular polygons.

title: Compute Perimeter of a Rectangle
description: The perimeter of a rectangle is found by adding twice the length and twice the width.
condition: Given a rectangle with known length and width.
response: Compute $2(\text{length} + \text{width})$ or add all four sides.
label: Principle / Model
example: If length = 5 in and width = 4 in, perimeter is $2(5 + 4) = 18$ inches.
notes: Equivalent to adding all four sides individually.

title: Create Appropriate Graph Scale
description: Determine and apply a consistent scale for a bar graph axis based on the data range.
condition: A blank or incomplete bar graph requires a numerical scale that fits the given data values.
response: Select equal intervals (e.g., by 5s or 10s) that cover the maximum data value and label the axis.
label: Principle
example: If the greatest value is 65, choose a scale such as $0, 10, 20, \dots, 70$.

title: Definition of Quadrilateral
description: Recall that a quadrilateral is a polygon with four sides.
condition: Asked for the meaning or property of a quadrilateral.
response: State that a quadrilateral has four sides.
label: Fact
example: A quadrilateral is a polygon with 4 sides.

title: Determine if Four Sides Are Equal
description: Check whether a quadrilateral has four sides of equal length.
condition: Side lengths of a quadrilateral are given or shown.
response: Compare all four side lengths and determine whether they are equal.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If a quadrilateral has side lengths 1 in, 1 in, 1 in, 1 in → conclude all four sides are equal.
notes: Supports classification as square or rhombus.

title: Determine Missing Side from Area
description: Find a missing side length of a rectangle using the area and one known side.
condition: Given area and one side length of a rectangle.
response: Divide area by known side to find the missing side.
label: Principle / Model
example: If area $=36$ and length $=6$, then width $= 36 \div 6 = 6$.

title: Distinguish Rhombus from Square
description: Recognize that while all squares are rhombuses, not all rhombuses are squares because right angles are not required.
condition: Given the claim that every rhombus is a square.
response: State that the claim is false and explain that rhombuses do not require four right angles.
label: Concept
example: A rhombus has four equal sides, but its angles do not have to be $90^\circ$, so not every rhombus is a square.

title: Draw Non-Square with Parallel Sides
description: Create a quadrilateral that has at least one pair of parallel sides but does not have all properties of a square.
condition: Asked to draw a quadrilateral with at least one pair of parallel sides that is not a square.
response: Draw a trapezoid or non-square parallelogram with at least one pair of parallel sides.
label: Production / Skill
example: Draw a trapezoid with one pair of parallel sides, such as vertices at (0,0), (4,0), (3,3), (1,3).

title: Ensure Shape Is Not a Rhombus
description: Construct or identify a quadrilateral that does not have four equal sides.
condition: Task requires a quadrilateral with at least one pair of parallel sides that is not a rhombus.
response: Provide or select a quadrilateral with parallel sides where not all four sides are equal (e.g., a non-square rectangle).
label: Rule / Plan
example: Draw a rectangle with side lengths 2 and 4. It has parallel sides but not all sides are equal, so it is not a rhombus.
notes: Requires satisfying one condition while excluding another.

title: Find Difference Between Categories
description: Determine how many more or fewer by subtracting one category value from another.
condition: Two numerical values are read from a graph and the problem asks how many more or fewer.
response: Subtract the smaller value from the larger value.
label: Principle
example: If tacos = 160 and pizza = 100, then difference = $160 - 100 = 60$.

title: Find Perimeter from Area and One Side
description: Determine the missing side of a rectangle using area and one side, then compute the perimeter.
condition: Given area of a rectangle and one side length.
response: Divide area by known side to find missing side, then compute perimeter using 2l + 2w.
label: Principle / Model
example: Area = 24 sq ft, length = 6 ft. Width = 24 ÷ 6 = 4 ft. Perimeter = 2(6) + 2(4) = 12 + 8 = 20 ft.

title: Find Side Length of Regular Polygon from Perimeter
description: Determine the side length of a regular polygon by dividing the perimeter by the number of equal sides.
condition: Given the total perimeter of a regular polygon and the number of equal sides.
response: Divide the perimeter by the number of sides.
label: Principle / Model
example: A regular hexagon has perimeter $54$. Since it has $6$ sides, each side is $54 \div 6 = 9$ inches.

title: Identify a Quadrilateral by Side Count
description: Recognize that a polygon with exactly four sides is a quadrilateral.
condition: A polygon is shown and the task is to determine whether it is a quadrilateral.
response: Count the sides; if the polygon has exactly 4 sides, classify it as a quadrilateral.
label: Rule / Plan
example: A shape with 4 sides (e.g., a trapezoid) → classify as a quadrilateral.
notes: Definition-based; relies on memorized meaning of quadrilateral.

title: Identify a Right Angle
description: Recognize a 90° angle in a polygon.
condition: An angle in a shape is shown and the task is to determine whether it is a right angle.
response: Determine whether the angle measures 90° (forms a square corner).
label: Category
example: A corner shaped like the corner of a square → identify as a right angle.
notes: Often based on visual recognition rather than measurement.

title: Identify Hour from Hour Hand
description: Determine the hour shown on an analog clock by locating the hour hand.
condition: Given an analog clock with an hour hand pointing to or between hour numbers.
response: State the hour indicated by the hour hand (the last number passed).
label: Production / Skill
example: If the hour hand is just past 11, the hour is $11$.
notes: Often used together with minute-hand identification.

title: Identify Minutes from Minute Hand
description: Determine the minutes shown on an analog clock by interpreting the minute hand position.
condition: Given an analog clock with the minute hand pointing to a number or tick mark.
response: Multiply the number the minute hand points to by 5 (or count tick marks) to state the minutes.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the minute hand points to $9$, then $9 \times 5 = 45$ minutes.

title: Identify Number of Sides in a Hexagon
description: A hexagon is defined as a polygon with six sides.
condition: Asked for the number of sides in a hexagon.
response: State that a hexagon has 6 sides.
label: Fact
example: A hexagon has 6 sides.
notes: Terminology fact that must be memorized.

title: Interpret Bar Height
description: Read the numerical value represented by the height or length of a bar using the graph scale.
condition: A bar is shown aligned to a labeled numerical axis.
response: State the numerical value corresponding to the top/end of the bar.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a bar reaches the tick mark labeled 90, then the value is $90$.

title: Interpret Pictograph Key
description: Use the pictograph key to determine how many units each symbol represents.
condition: A pictograph includes a key stating that one symbol equals a fixed number of items.
response: Multiply the number of symbols by the value of one symbol.
label: Principle
example: If each circle represents 10 students and there are 6 circles, then $6 \times 10 = 60$ students.

title: Match Analog Clock to Digital Time
description: Match a clock face to its corresponding written digital time.
condition: Given an analog clock and a list of digital times.
response: Select the digital time that matches the hour and minute shown on the clock.
label: Production / Skill
example: A clock showing hour $1$ and minute $55$ matches $1{:}55$.

title: Measure to Nearest Quarter Inch
description: Measure an object and record its length rounded to the nearest quarter inch.
condition: Given an object aligned to a ruler with quarter-inch marks.
response: State the length rounded to the nearest $\tfrac{1}{4}$ inch.
label: Production / Skill
example: A pencil slightly past $2\tfrac{1}{4}$ inches is recorded as $2\tfrac{1}{4}$ inches.
notes: Involves visual estimation and rounding. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Plot X on Line Plot
description: Place an X above the correct value on a number line to represent one data point.
condition: A numerical measurement is given and a line plot with matching scale is provided.
response: Mark an X above the corresponding number on the line plot.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a pencil measures $2\frac{1}{4}$ inches, place an X above $2.25$ (or $2\frac{1}{4}$) on the number line.

title: Properties of a Rectangle
description: Recall that a rectangle has four right angles and two pairs of parallel sides; opposite sides are equal.
condition: A shape is identified as a rectangle or the term rectangle is given.
response: State that it has 4 right angles and opposite sides are equal and parallel.
label: Fact
example: A rectangle has four right angles and two pairs of parallel sides.
notes: Used to evaluate truth of statements.

title: Properties of a Rhombus
description: Recall that a rhombus is a quadrilateral with four sides of equal length.
condition: A shape is identified as a rhombus or the term rhombus is given.
response: State that all four sides are equal in length.
label: Fact
example: A rhombus has 4 sides of equal length.

title: Properties of a Square
description: Recall that a square has four equal sides and four right angles.
condition: A shape is identified as a square or the term square is given.
response: State that it has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
label: Fact
example: A square has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
notes: Combines properties of rectangle and rhombus.

title: Recognize At Least One Pair of Parallel Sides
description: Determine whether a quadrilateral has at least one pair of parallel sides.
condition: A quadrilateral is shown and the task asks whether it has at least one pair of parallel sides.
response: Inspect opposite sides and determine whether at least one pair does not intersect and remains equidistant (parallel).
label: Rule / Plan
example: In a rectangle, the top and bottom sides are parallel → it has at least one pair of parallel sides.
notes: Students may rely on visual recognition of parallelism.

title: Same Perimeter, Different Area
description: Generate rectangle dimensions that produce a given perimeter but a different area.
condition: Given a required perimeter and requirement for different area than a known rectangle.
response: Choose length and width values whose perimeter matches the given value and verify the area is different.
label: Principle / Model
example: If perimeter must be 20 ft, choose 5 ft by 5 ft. Perimeter = 2(5)+2(5)=20; Area = 25 sq ft, which differs from 24 sq ft.

title: Set Equal Areas to Find Missing Side
description: When two rectangles have equal areas, set their area expressions equal and solve for the unknown side length.
condition: Given two rectangles with equal area, one with a missing side length.
response: Compute known area and divide by known side length to find the missing side.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one rectangle has area $6 \times 4 = 24$ and another has width 3 m, then its length is $24 \div 3 = 8$ m.

title: Square as a Special Rhombus
description: Recognize that a square meets the defining properties of a rhombus.
condition: Asked whether every square is a rhombus and to justify.
response: State that yes, because both have 4 equal sides and 2 pairs of parallel sides.
label: Principle / Model
example: A square has 4 equal sides and 2 pairs of parallel sides. A rhombus is defined as a quadrilateral with 4 equal sides and 2 pairs of parallel sides. Therefore, every square is a rhombus.

title: Subtract Duration to Find Start Time
description: Find a start time by subtracting a given number of minutes from a known end time.
condition: Given an end time and a duration in minutes.
response: Subtract the duration from the end time, regrouping across the hour if needed.
label: Principle / Model
example: $4{:}45$ p.m. minus $38$ minutes: subtract $45 - 38 = 7$, so start time is $4{:}07$ p.m.
